Facts.text: 7th recorded perihelion passage of Halley's Comet
Liberius begins his reign as Catholic Pope replacing Julius I
St Adrian III begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Edward Stafford, 3rd Duke of Buckingham, is executed for treason
Battle at Zabern: duke of Lutherans beats rebels
Afghan leader Sher Shah Suri crowned ruler of Hindustan
Artois, Henegouwen and French-Flanders sign Treaty, the Peace of Parma recognizing Spanish Duke of Parma as land guardian
Anne of Denmark is crowned Queen of Scotland
First merry-go-round is seen at a fair in Philippopolis in the Ottoman Empire
Italian Jesuit Niccolo Zucchi, 1st to see 2 belts on Jupiter surface
Earl Johann Tilly attacks Maagdenburg
Emperor Ferdinand III defeats Maximilian I of Bavaria
Frontenac becomes Governor of New France (Canada)
Frenchmen Louis Jolliet and Jacques Marquette, a Jesuit, set out  from the Saint-Ignace mission on an expedition to explore the Mississippi river (reach border of Louisiana and Arkansas)
Pirates sack Velacruz, New Spain, taking 4,000 prisoners for ransom
Maximilian Emanuel of Bavaria honored as "sovereign of Netherlands"
Great Britain passes Molasses Act, putting high tariffs on rum and molasses imported to the colonies from a country other than British possessions
French army takes Austrian Netherlands
-18] Tax revolt in Gorinchem
Britain declares war on France (7 Years' or French & Indian War)
American Revolutionary War: the Continental Congress bans trade with Canada
English slave ship Sisters, en route from Africa to Cuba, capsizes killing hundreds
Twenty-four merchants form the New York Stock Exchange at 70 Wall Street
Hard frost in southern New England
John Hawkins and Richard French patent the reaping machine
Papal States annexed by France
Denmark cedes Norway to Sweden (Constitution Day)
Norwegian constitution passed by constituent assembly at Eidsvoll
Occupation of Monaco changes from French to Austrian
Gerrit, Count Schimmelpenninck resigns as Chairman of the Council of Ministers of Netherlands
Fire destroy Centrum in St Louis, Missouri
Thorbeckes liberals win 2nd-Parliamentary election
Australian Rules Football first 'laws of the game' published
German football club TSV 1860 München is founded
Battle of Princeton West Virginia, ends with about 128 causalities
Battle of Big Black River Bridge, Mississippi
Rosalía de Castro publishes Cantares Gallegos, her first book in the Galician language
Battle of Adairsville Georgia, Union forces Confederates to retreat
The International Telegraph Union (later the International Telecommunication Union) is established
Bohemian Club incorporated in San Francisco
1st Kentucky Derby: Oliver Lewis aboard Aristides wins in 2:37.75
Edwin T. Holmes installs the first telephone switchboard burglar alarm
Revised version of New Testament
Alaska becomes a US territory
Comic Cuts, 1st weekly comic paper, published in London
Pietro Mascagni's opera "Rustic Chivalry" premieres in Rome at the Teatro Costanzi
Camp Merritt forms in Presidio [see 0503]
Victoria & Albert Museum foundation laid, London, England
British troops relieve Mafeking (Cape Colony)
In China, three villages within 100 miles of Peking are burned by Boxers and 60 Chinese Christians killed
Greek archaeologist Valerios Stais discovers the Antikythera mechanism, an ancient mechanical analog computer
Cleveland Indians beat NY Highlanders 9-2 in Columbus Ohio
Waseda U of Tokyo defeats LA High School 5-3 in baseball
White firemen on the Georgia Railroad strike to protest the hiring of Black workers
Canada sets the designs for the 1-50 cent coins
National Baptist Convention chartered
British Summer Time (Daylight Savings) introduced
Maurice Ravel's orchestral piece "Alborada del gracioso" premieres in Paris by the Pasdeloup Orchestra
UK War Department orders use of National Star Insignia on all airplanes
First flight by Dutch airline KLM (Koninklijke Luchtvaart Maatschappij), a joint venture with British Aerial Transport; a de Havilland double-decker plane from London lands at Schiphol in Amsterdam
Belgian and Luxembourg sign customs union
Fire during closing day ceremonies at Grover Cleveland School, South Carolina
MLB Chicago Cubs beat Boston Braves, 4-3, in 22 innings, at Braves Field in Boston, Massachusetts
US Army aviation pioneer Major Harold Geiger dies in the crash of his Airco DH.4 de Havilland plane at Olmsted Field, Pennsylvania
US Congress changes name "Porto Rico" to "Puerto Rico"
Juan Negrin succeeds Largo Caballero as Spain's premier
Radio quiz show "Information Please!" debuts on NBC Blue Network
US Congress approves Vinson Naval Act, which funds a two-ocean navy
First televised baseball game is broadcast on NBC, with Princeton University defeating Columbia University 2-1
Germany occupies Brussels, Belgium and begins invasion of France
Nazis bombs Middelburg/B IJzerdrat begins illegal defiance
Pennsylvania declares legal holiday to honor A's manager Connie Mack
The United States Army contracts with the University of Pennsylvania's Moore School to develop the ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator And Computer).
World War II: the Dambuster Raids by No. 617 Squadron RAF on German dams
-18] Allied air raid on Surabaja, Java
Chinese and US armed forces take Myitkyina Airport, Burma
Operation Straightline: Allies land in Dutch New Guinea
2 US P-47 Thunderbolts bomb Kiushu
KVP Labor/Communists win 1st post-WW2 Dutch parliamentary elections
US President Harry Truman seizes control of nation's railroads to delay a strike
Israel liberates Acre, Nebi Yusha & Telel-Kadi
Soviet Union recognizes Israel
British government recognises Republic of Ireland (previously Irish Free State)
Yanks & Browns use record 41 players in a game
US Supreme Court unanimously rules on Brown v Topeka Board of Education reverses 1896 "separate but equal" Plessy v Ferguson decision ruling racial segregation in public schools as illegal
Dutch government of Willem Drees resigns
Prayer Pilgrimage, biggest civil rights demonstration to date (DC)
School desegregation law, Brown v Board of education
Emergency crisis proclaimed in Algeria
First atomic reactor system patented by J. W. Flora of Canoga Park, California
Columbia Records officially opens the Pitman Pressing Plant, designed by American architect Minoru Yamasaki, in Pitman, New Jersey
Marin County withdraws from BART district
Houston Colt .45's Don Notterbart no-hits Phillies, 4-1
US performs nuclear Test at Nevada Test Site
Phillies triple play Houston Colt .45s
KFDO (now KVIJ) TV channel 8 in Sayre, Oklahoma (ABC) begins broadcasting
Dylan's 1965 UK Tour is released as film "Don't Look Back"
European Space Research Org launches 1st satellite
Frank Howard belts record 8th HR in 5th straight game
US performs nuclear Test at Nevada Test Site
"My Wife, My Dog, My Cat" by Maskman & The Agents hits #92
Baltimore, Cleveland & Pittsburgh agree to go from NFC to AFC in NFL
Russian probe Venera 6 landed on Venus
Stephen Schwartz' musical "Godspell" premieres off-Broadway
Washington State bans sex discrimination
Netherlands & People's Republic of China exchange ambassadors

The Irish Republican Army (IRA) fires on workers leaving the Mackies engineering works in west Belfast (Although the factory was sited in a Catholic area it had an almost entirely Protestant workforce)
Angels outfielder Bobby Valentine breaks his leg trying to scale wall to prevent a Dick Green home run during a 5-4 loss to the A's
Five British Army soldiers are killed by a Provisional Irish Republican Army booby-trap bomb in Omagh, County Tyrone
Joe Ferguson, hits the 6,000th Dodger home run
US performs 3 nuclear tests at Rifle Colorado
US Senate Watergate Committee begins its hearings into the break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters at the Watergate office complex in Washington, D.C.

Symbionese Liberation Army shoot-out with Los Angeles police kills six SLA members in the gunfire and resulting fire. One of the largest police shootouts in US history, with more than 9,000 rounds fired.
Ulster Volunteer Force (UVF) explode four bombs in Republic of Ireland, killing 33 civilians, wounding 300 (highest number of casualties in a single incident during "The Troubles")
"Funky Gibbon" by The Goodies hits #79
10CC releases "I'm Not in Love"
NBC pays $5M for rights to show "Gone With The Wind" one time
Earthquake in Uzbekistan: thousands killed
Los Angeles Dodgers Lee Lacy hits record 3rd consecutive pinch-hit home run
Coldest temperature ever recorded in Hawaii: 12°F (-11°C), on the Big Island at Mauna Kea Observatory, elevation 13,796 feet
MLB Philadelphia Phillies win over Cubs, 23-22 in 10 innings at Wrigley Field on a breezy day in Chicago; teams combine for 13 first inning runs, and finish game with 50 hits, including 11 homeruns
Major race riot in Miami, Florida - 16 killed, 300 injured
V. S. Kumar Anandan from Sri Lanka balances on one foot for 33 hours (Guinness World Record)
Islanders score 5 power play goals against Flyers in a playoff
Israel & Lebanon sign a peace treaty
Stanley Cup Final, Nassau Coliseum, Uniondale, NY: 4 consecutive titles for NY Islanders; sweep Edmonton Oilers in 4 games with a 4-2 Game 4 win
Cincinnati Reds Mario Soto throws 4 strikeouts in one inning
Marvin Creamer becomes 1st known person to sail around the world without navigational instruments, arriving back in Cape May, New Jersey, after 513 days
Les Anderson, catches record 97 lb 4 oz Chinook salmon, while fishing off coast of Alaska
"Chicken Song" by Spitting Image hits #1 on the UK pop chart
USS Stark hit by Iraqi missiles, 37 sailors die
Longest cab ride at 14,000 miles costs $16,000 (later surpassed)
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits a record 2,831.71
European court rules on pension rights for men & women
WIBC Bowling Queens won by Patty Ann
World Health Organization takes homosexuality out of its list of mental illnesses
At least 10 people are killed as pro-democracy protests begin in Thailand
Bakili Muluzi's UDF wins Malawi presidents/parliamentary election
Habib & Whitaker make 320 for 5th Cricket wkt, Leics v Worcs
WIBC Bowling Queens won by Sandra Jo Shiery-Odom
New York Yankees pitcher David Wells tosses a perfect game in a 4-0 win against the Minnesota Twins at Yankee Stadium, NY

Explosion rocks Glorietta Mall in Manila's Makati business district, injuring 13 people, mostly teenagers. According to local authorities, the homemade bomb was placed in front of a toilet beside a video arcade.
Massachusetts becomes the first U.S. state to legalize same-sex marriage
Aircraft carrier USS Oriskany is sunk in the Gulf of Mexico to form an artificial reef
Demolition work begins at Dublin's famous Lansdowne Road Stadium to be replaced by the new Aviva Stadium, opened in 2010
Trains from North and South Korea cross the 38th Parallel in a test run agreed upon by both governments, the first trains to cross the Demilitarized Zone since 1953
Video game Minecraft is first released to the public while still in development
Archaeologists announce the discovery of the oldest tomb in Mesoamerica at 2,700 years old, at Chiapa de Corzo, Mexico, containing the remains of a 50-year-old man
90 people are killed and 200 are injured after a series of bombings across Iraq
Atlético Madrid win the 2013–14 La Liga
Scottish Earl of Lennox Matthew Stewart signs a secret treaty with English KingHenry VIII
English KingWilliam IIIdeclares war on France in support of the Dutch Republic (Nine Years' War)
Italian composerAntonio Vivaldi's first opera "Ottone in villa" opens at Teatro delle Grazie in Vicenza, Republic of Venice (now Italy)
Battle of Chotusitz: Prussia led byFrederick the Greatdefeats Austria
The diaries ofLord Byronare burnt by six of the poet's friends at the office of John Murray in London, sometimes described as “the greatest crime in literary history”
7th US Cavalry under GeneralGeorge Armstrong Custerleaves Fort Lincoln
Frederick Douglassappointed recorder of deeds for the District of Columbia
Clyde Fitch's play "Beau Brummel" premieres in NYC
The first successful submarine that can run submerged for any considerable distance and combines electric and gasoline engines is launched in the USA by its designerJohn Philip Holland
"The Wonderful Wizard of Oz" is first published byL. Frank Baumwith illustrations by William Wallace Denslow in Chicago
Maurice Ravel's song cycle "Shéhérazade" premieres with Jeanne Hatto as soprano, at the Salle Nouveau Théâtre, Paris, France
Last liberal British government ofH. H. Asquithfalls
US PresidentWarren G. Hardingopens (via telephone) the 1st Valencia Orange Show in Anaheim, California
Cleveland IndianTris Speakergets his 3,000th hit
Chiang Kai-shekis made supreme warlord in Canton
Wilhelm Marx succeedsHans Lutheras Chancellor of Germany
Vidkun Quislingand Johan Bernhard Hjort form Nasjonal Samling, the national-socialist party of Norway
TheGlenn MillerOrchestra begins a three month engagement at the Glen Island Casino in New Rochelle, New York
Dutch SS vows loyalty toHitler
MillionaireHoward Hughescrashes into Lake Mead, while test flying his Sikorsky S-43, killing CAA inspector Ceco Cline and Richard Felt
10th Cannes Film Festival: "Friendly Persuasion" directed byWilliam Wylerwins the Palme d'Or
Sam Sneadsets PGA record for 36 holes at 122
Fidel Castrooffers to exchange Bay of Pigs prisoners for 500 bulldozers
FirstTim Horton's coffee and donut shop opens in Hamilton, Ontario by NHL player Tim Horton
Anonymous buyer purchases one of the pairs of ruby slippers worn byJudy Garlandin "The Wizard of Oz" for $15,000 at MGM Studio auction, and donates them to the Smithsonian Institution
Hank Aaronbecomes 9th player to get 3,000 hits
Stevie Wonderreleases the music single "You Are The Sunshine Of My Life", goes to #1 and wins him a Grammy Award for Best Male Pop Vocal Performance
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes his 15th String quartet
"Rolling Stones"singerMick Jagger(31) punches a restaurant window, needs 20 stitches for repairs
28th Emmy Awards: "TheMary Tyler MooreShow, Jack Albertson & Michael Learned win
Menachem Begin's Likud Party wins election in Israel
Nolan Bushnellopens the 1st Chuck E. Cheese's Pizza Time Theatre restaurant in  San Jose, California
Paul &Linda McCartneyappear in remote segment from England, after being awakened by Father Guido Sarducci, on "Saturday Night Live"

Prince Charlescalls proposed addition to the National Gallery, London, a "monstrous carbuncle on the face of a much-loved and elegant friend," sparking controversy on the role of the Royal Family and course of modern architecture.
Nelson Mandela(70) receives a BA from University of South Africa
"Cheers" starKelsey Grammersentenced to jail for 30 days for DWI
The Bank of China Tower in Hong Kong designed byI. M. Peiopens as the first supertall skyscraper outside the US
ExposGary Carteris 3rd to catch 2,000 games (joins Boone & Fisk)
"Chattahoochee" single released byAlan Jackson(CMA Award Single of the Year & Billboard Song of the Year, 1993)
Intel's new Pentium processor is unveiled
Troops loyal toLaurent-Désiré Kabilamarch into Kinshasa and seize power, ending the First Congo War
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushcalls for reduced regulations to encourage more oil, gas, and nuclear production
Australian singerKylie Minogueis diagnosed with breast cancer at 36
Dale Jarrett's final race, the All-Star race, before joining ESPN's NASCAR coverage full-time
Bayern Munich defeats Borussia Dortmund to win the 2013–14 DFB-Pokal
Center-right Hindu nationalist party, the BJP, led byNarendra Modi, wins a landslide general election victory in India
Anne Boleyn's four "lovers" are executed shortly before her own beheading
The first color photograph, of a tartan ribbon is shown by Scottish scientistJames Clerk Maxwellto the Royal Institution in London
US GeneralDwight Eisenhowersets D-Day for June 5
Sanctuary of Christ the King inaugurated, a 28 meter (92 ft) high monument and shrine overlooking Lisbon, Portugal by sculptor Francisco Franco de Sousa
Jien, Japanese poet and historian, born in Kyoto (d. 1225)
Edmund, Earl of Rutland, brother of Kings Edward IV of England and Richard III of England, born in Rouen, Normandy (d. 1460)
Engelbert II, Count of Nassau-Dillenburg-Dietz and viceroy of Luxembourg, born in Breda, Netherlands (d. 1504)
Martin Delrio, Flemish theologian and occultist, born in Antwerp (d. 1608)
Ferdinand Charles, Archduke of Austria and Regent of the Tyrol, born in Innsbruck, Austria (d. 1662)
Andreas Felix von Oefele, German historian and librarian, born in Munich (d. 1780)
Robert Darcy, 4th Earl of Holderness, English diplomat and politician, born in England (d. 1778)
Francesco Pasquale Ricci, Italian composer, born in Como, Duchy of Milan, Habsburg Empire (d. 1817)
John Penn, American attorney (signed US Declaration of Independence), born in Port Royal, Caroline County, Virginia (d. 1788)
Seth Warner, American revolutionary leader, born in Roxbury, Connecticut (d. 1784)
John St Aubyn, British fossil collector, born in Golden Square, London (d. 1839)
Caroline of Brunswick, Queen Consort of King George IV (1820-21), born in Brunswick, Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1821)
Henry Paget, 1st Marquess of Anglesey, English general, born in London (d. 1854)
Anna Brownell Jameson, Anglo-Irish historian and writer (Loves of the Poets), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1860)
Martinus Niewindt, Dutch Bishop of Curacao (advocate for fair treatment and abolition of slavery), born in Amsterdam (d. 1860)
Carl Friedrich Zöllner, German composer, born in Mittelhausen, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1860)
Robert Smith Surtees, British novelist (Jorrocks’s Jaunts and Jollities), born in The Riding, England (d. 1864)
Ottho Gerhard Heldring, Dutch preacher and reformer (Heldring Institutions), born in Zevenaar, Netherlands (d. 1876)
Charles-Louis-Adolphe Vogel, French composer (Les trois couleurs), born in Lille, France (d. 1892)
Joseph Warren Revere, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1880)
Johann Nepomuk Kafka, Bohemian pianist and composer, born in Neustadt an der Mettau, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1886)
Sebastian Kneipp, Bavarian priest and naturopathist (known for "Kneipp Cure" form of hydrotherapy), born in Stephansried, Germany (d. 1897)
Charlotte Sainton-Dolby, English contralto and composer, born in London (d. 1885)
Sir Norman Lockyer, English physicist, co-founder of helium gas, founder and editor of "Nature" magazine, born in Rugby, Warwickshire (d. 1920)
Virginie Loveling, Flemish writer (Sophie) and poet, born in Nevele, East Flanders, Belgium (d. 1923)
Wilhelm Steinitz, Austrian World Chess Champion (1866-94), born in Prague, Bohemia, Austrian Empire (d. 1900)
Julius Wellhausen, German biblical scholar (analysis of the structure and dating of the Pentateuch), born in Hamelin, Hanover, Germany (d. 1918)
Edmund Bishop, English Roman Catholic historian and Secretary of Thomas Carlyle, born in Totnes, England (d. 1917)
Antonio Scontrino, Italian composer, born in Trapani, Sicily, The Kingdom of Two Sicilies (d. 1922)
Austin Lane Crothers, American politician (Governor of Maryland, 1908-12), born in Conowingo, Maryland (d. 1912)
Erik Satie, French composer (Gymnopédies), born in Honfleur, Normandy (d. 1925)
Gerrit Mannoury, Dutch mathematician and philosopher, born in Wormerveer, Netherlands (d. 1956)
Horace Elgin Dodge, American automobile manufacturing pioneer (co-founder of Dodge Brothers Company), born in Niles, Michigan (d. 1920)
Henk Bremmer, Dutch art historian (Modern Kunstwerken), born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1956)
Dorothy M. Richardson, English novelist (Pilgrimage), born in Abington, Berkshire (d. 1957)
Henri Barbusse, French novelist (Le Feu), born in Asnieres, France (d. 1935)
Alban Collignon, Belgian sport journalist, born in Mechelen, Belgium (d. 1955)
Pascual de Rogatis, Argentine composer (Huemac; Estampas Argentinas), born in Teora, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1980)
Selmer Jackson, American actor (The Grapes of Wrath, Life & Legend of Wyatt Earp), born in Lake Mills, Iowa (d. 1971)
Tich Freeman, English cricket leg-spin bowler (12 Tests, 66 wickets; Kent CCC), born in Lewisham, England (d. 1965)
Alfonso Reyes, Mexican writer (Simpatías y diferencias) and diplomat (Higenia Cruel), born in Monterrey, Mexico (d. 1959)
Philip James, American composer, and conductor (New Jersey Symphony, 1922-29; Bamberger Little Symphony, 1929-36), and educator (NYU, 1934-56), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1975)
Princess Alexandra, 2nd Duchess of Fife, granddaughter of King Edward VII and great-granddaughter of Queen Victoria, born in East Sheen Lodge, Richmond, England (d. 1959)
Frederick McKinley Jones, American inventor (developments in refrigeration), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1961)
Brasílio Itiberê da Cunha Luz, Brazilian composer, born in Curitiba, State of Paraná, Brazil (d. 1967)
Hannah Tillich, German-American writer (Harbor Mouse) and wife of Paul Tillich, born in Rotenburg an der Fulda, Germany (d. 1988)
Odd Hassel, Norwegian chemist (Nobel 1969 - the study of the three-dimensional geometric structure of molecules), born in Kristiana, Norway (d. 1981)
A. J. Casson, Canadian painter (member of Group of Seven), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1992)
Ralf Harolde, American actor (Framed, Smart Money, Tip-off), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1974)
Nicolai Berezowsky, Russian-American composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1953)
Werner Egk, German composer (Die Zaubergeige), born in Donauwörth, Germany (d. 1983)
John Vincent, American composer, born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 1977)
Maurice Kusell, American film choreographer (Puttin' On the Ritz), born in Champaign, Illinois (d. 1992)
Jean Gabin, French actor (Moulin Rouge, La Grande Illusion), born in Paris, France (d. 1976)
John Patrick, American screenwriter and playwright (Pulitzer Prize 1954), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1995)
Eric Mensforth, British pioneer in the development of the helicopter in Britain, born in Stretford, Manchester (d. 2000)
Horace McMahon, American actor (Detective Story, Martin Kane Private Eye), born in South Norwalk, Connecticut (d. 1971)
Zinka Milanov (née Kunc), Croatian soprano (Ljubljana Opera, 1927-34; NY Metropolitan Opera, 1937-66), born in Zagreb, Croatia (d. 1989)
Ilona Elek, Hungarian fencer (Olympic gold 1936, 48), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1988)
Sigismund Toduţă, Romanian composer, born in Simeria, Romania (d. 1991)
Edward Playfair, British senior civil servant, born in London (d. 1999)
Karl Schäfer, Austrianfigure skater(Olympic gold 1932, 36), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1976)
Magda Schneider, German actress (Eva, Going Gay, Be Mine Tonight), born in Bavaria, German (d. 1996)
Lisa Fonssagrives, Swedish supermodel, born in Västra Götaland County, Sweden (d. 1992)
Maureen O'Sullivan, Irish actress (Tarzan, Pride & Prejudice, Hannah and Her Sisters), born in Boyle, County Roscommon, Ireland (d. 1998)
Archibald Cox, American lawyer and special prosecutor (Watergate), born in Plainfield, New Jersey (d. 2004)
Clarence "Ace" Parker, American NFL quarterback (Brooklyn, Boston Yanks), born in Portsmouth, Virginia (d. 2013)
Sándor Végh, Hungarian-French violinist teacher and conductor, born in Kolozsvár, Transylvania, Austria-Hungary (d. 1997)
Desmond Barel Hirshfield, Lord Hirshfield, British chartered accountant, born in Birmingham (d. 1993)
Guido Masanetz, German composer (In Frisco ist der Teufel Los), born in Friedeck, Austria-Hungary (d. 2015)
Gus Viseur, Belgian-French jazz button accordionist, born in Lessines, Belgium (d. 1974)
John Hinde, English pioneering color photographer (color postcards, documented circus life), born in Somerset  (d. 1997)[1]
(Märta) Birgit Nilsson, Swedish operatic soprano (Elektra; Salome), born in Västra Karup, Skåne, Sweden (d. 2005)
Antonio Aguilar [Barraza], Mexican singer, producer and actor (Emiliano Zapata) who popularized the Mexican equestrian sport la charrería, born in Villanueva, Zacatecas, Mexico (d. 2007)
Merle Miller, American author and gay rights pioneer (That Winter, A Gay and Melancholy Sound), born in Montour, Iowa (d. 1986)[1][2]
Harriet Van Horne, American columnist and panelist (Leave it to Girls), born in Syracuse, New York (d. 1998)
Lydia Wideman, Finnish cross country skier (first female cross-country Olympic medalist, gold 10k 1952), born in Vippula, Finland (d. 2019)
Bob Merrill, Americancomposerandlyricist(People, Mambo Italiano), born in Atlantic City, New Jersey (d. 1998)
Dennis Brain, British classical horn player (Serenade), born in London, England (d. 1957)
Michael Beetham, British Marshall of the RAF (Falklands War), born in London, England (d. 2015)
Peter Mennin [Mennini], American composer (Moby Dick; The Cycle), and educator (Juilliard, 1962-83), born in Erie, Pennsylvania (d. 1983)
Francis Tombs, Lord Tombs, British engineer and businessman (CEO of Rolls Royce), born in Walsall, England (d. 2020)[1]
Frantisek Kovaricek, Czech composer (Ukradeny mesic (The Stolen Moon)), born in Liteniny, Czechoslovakia (d. 2003)
Cicely Berry, British theatre director (Royal Shakespeare Company) and voice coach, born in Berkhamsted, England (d. 2018)[1]
David Ogilvy, Earl of Airlie, Scottish peer, Lord Chamberlain to Queen Elizabeth, born in Westminster, England (d. 2023)
Geoffrey Caston, British academic administrator (University of Oxford, 1972-79; Vice-Chancellor of University of the South Pacific, 1983-92), born in London (d. 2018)
Tenniel Evans, British actor and clergyman (10 Rillington Place, My Brother's Keeper), born in Nairobi, Kenya (d. 2009)
Donald Cameron Watt, British historian (How War Came: The Immediate Origins of the Second World War, 1938–1939), born in Rugby, England (d. 2014)
Rose Leiman Goldemberg, American playwright and screenwriter (The Burning Bed, Stone Pillow), born in Staten Island, New York (d. 2025)
George Weinberg, American psychotherapist (coined "homophobia"), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2017)
John Davies, Australian swimmer (Olympic gold 200m breaststroke 1952; WR 200m breaststroke 2:34.4 1952), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2020)
Raymond Hide, British geophysicist (geomagnetism, meteorology, geodesy and oceanography), born in London (d. 2016)
Frank Price, American studio executive and head of Columbia Pictures (1979-83; 1990-91), born in Decatur, Illinois (d. 2025)[1]
Marshall Applewhite, American cult leader (Heaven's Gate), born in Spur, Texas (d. 1997)
Stan Albeck, American basketball coach (Cleveland Cavaliers, San Antonio Spurs, New Jersey Nets, Chicago Bulls), born in Chenoa, Illinois (d. 2021)
[Walter] Dewey Redman, American jazz saxophonist, born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 2006)
Colin Hope, British industrialist (Turner and Newall), born in Birkenhead (d. 2015)
Peter Burge, Australian cricket batsman (42 Tests, 4 x 100, HS 181; Queensland CA), born in Brisbane, Australia (d. 2001)
Rodric Braithwaite, British author (Afgantsy, Moscow 1941) and former ambassador to USSR, born in London
Abe Terry, English rugby league front rower (11 Tests; Challenge Cup 1961 St. Helens RLFC [216 games]), born in St. Helens, England (d. 2024)
Earl Morrall, American NFL quarterback (Super Bowl 1970, 72, 73; Pro Bowl 1957, 68; First-team All-Pro 1968, 72; NFL MVP 1968; Detroit Lions, Miami Dolphins), born in Muskegon, Michigan (d. 2014)
Dennis Potter, British TV dramatist (The Singing Detective, Karaoke, Midnight Movie), born in Gloucestershire, England (d. 1994)
Guy Camberabero, French rugby union flyhalf (14 Tests 110 points; ROC La Voulte Sportif, US Tyrosse	RUC), born in Saubion, France (d. 2023)
Lars Gustafsson, Swedish poet and novelist (The Death of a Beekeeper, The Cracks in the Wall), born in Västerås, Sweden (d. 2016)
Philippe Boesmans, Belgian pianist, and composer (Upon La-Mi; Reigen), born in Tongeren, Belgium (d. 2022)
Hazel R. O'Leary, American lawyer, politician, and 7th United States Secretary of Energy (1993-7), born in Newport News, Virginia
Don Dolan, actor (Guy Lewis- General Hospital), born in Staten Island, New York
Pervis Jackson, American pop and R&B singer (The Spinners, 1954-2008 - "Games People Play"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2008)
Trinus Riemersma, Dutch author and poet (Fabryk / Factory; De Reade Bwarre / The Red Dwarf), born in Ferwert, Netherlands (d. 2011)[1]
Carol Saline, American journalist, broadcaster and author (Sisters, Mothers and Daughters), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2025)
Gary Paulsen, American author of young adult literature, born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2021)
Gretchen Dow Simpson, American artist (New Yorker magazine covers, 1974-93), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 2025)[1]
Wim de Bie, Dutch comedian, satirist, and writer (van Kooten & de Bie - The Simplistic Covenant), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 2023)
Ben Nelson [Earl Benjamin Nelson], American Democratic politician, 37th governor of Nebraska and US senator (2001-13), born in McCook, Nebraska[1][2]
David Cope, American composer known his pioneering work using artificial intelligence to create music, born in San Francisco, California (d. 2025)[1]
Malcolm Hale, American pop musician (Spanky and Our Gang), born in Butte, Montana (d. 1968)
Myles Brand, American sports administrator (4th NCAA President), born in Brooklyn, NY (d. 2009)
Taj Mahal [Henry Saint Clair Fredericks, Jr.], American singer-songwriter (Real Thing), born in Harlem, New York
Rui Rodrigues, Portuguese soccer centre-back (12 caps; Académica, Benfica, Vitória Guimarães), born in Lourenço Marques, Portuguese Mozambique (d.2024)
